title: Elbow Fractures Overview -- Prognosis
content: The prognosis for most patients with supracondylar fractures is generally favorable. However, outcomes tend to worsen with increasing fracture severity. Traumatic nerve injuries are typically transient and often resolve spontaneously. [28]

title: Elbow Fractures Overview -- Etiology
content: Supracondylar fractures may present as either extension- or flexion-type injuries, depending on the direction of distal fragment displacement. Extension-type fractures typically result from a FOOSH injury, while flexion-type fractures commonly arise from a direct blow to the posterior olecranon. Radial head fractures commonly occur following a FOOSH injury with the forearm in pronation. [19]

title: Humeral Shaft Fractures -- Epidemiology
content: As mentioned, humeral shaft fractures comprise 1-5% of all fractures. These injuries have a bimodal age distribution, with common etiologies varying by age group. The vulnerable populations are individuals in the 3rd and 7th decades of life, with high-force trauma more common in younger people and low-impact injuries more frequent in older patients.

title: Distal Humerus Fractures -- Complications
content: Nonunion: The risk for nonunion is relatively rare but can occur in distal humerus fractures. Another thing to consider is the risk for non-union of an olecranon osteotomy site if the surgeon chooses to utilize this approach. Risk factors that are generally accepted in association with nonunion are smoking, obesity, and diabetes mellitus. [5] Nonunion can almost always be treated with revision open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) utilizing bone graft.

title: Forearm Fractures -- Prognosis
content: Overall, forearm fractures have a good prognosis with union rates of approximately 95% to 98%. It has been noted in various studies that the union rate is slightly higher in the case of radius fracture than ulnar fracture. [30] [31] [32] The plate osteosynthesis shows a slightly better outcome as compared to the third-generation intramedullary nail. In contrast, the first-generation intramedullary nail does not provide rotatory stability, and hence the outcome was poor compared to compressive plating.

content: In contrast, proximal shaft fractures are more likely to require surgical intervention due to their association with radial head instability. Open isolated ulnar shaft fractures also require a formal surgical debridement with internal fixation, as is the standard of care for open fractures. title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Introduction
content: Lateral humeral condyle fractures are the second most commonly encountered pediatric elbow fractures next to supracondylar fractures (see Image . Lateral Humeral Condyle Fracture). These injuries are unique, being intraarticular but vulnerable to displacement and nonunion owing to the forearm extensors' pull on the lateral epicondyle near the condylar area. These fractures can result in complications like elbow deformity if not appropriately managed. [1]

title: Humerus Fractures Overview -- Complications
content: Radial nerve palsy is a feared complication of humerus shaft fractures that can occur during the injury, open reduction, and internal fixation or intramedullary nailing. In closed fractures, this is often a result of neuropraxia, while in open fractures it is a result so neurogenesis. This complication is more common in third distal fractures as this is the location where the radial nerve is closest to the humerus. In a closed fracture, radial nerve palsy is not an indication for open reduction and internal fixation with nerve exploration. Most palsies return with no treatment in 3 to 6 months. If no return of function is seen at 3 to 4 months, an EMG should be performed. Surgical exploration is indicated in open fractures, a closed fracture with the failure of improvement over 3 to 6 months, and fibrillations at 3 to 4 months on EMG.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
content: Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm and fracture of upper limb are both classified under كسر الترقوة, indicating that the clavicle fracture serves as a broader parent category for these upper‑limb injury terms. In clinical practice, كسر الترقوة frequently co‑occurs with كسر الكتف (shoulder fracture) and كسر عظم الفخذ (femur fracture), reflecting a common pattern of multiple skeletal injuries in patients who sustain trauma to the clavicle. Recognizing that clavicle fractures are linked to both other upper‑limb fracture descriptors and to concurrent shoulder and femur fractures helps clinicians anticipate associated injuries, prioritize comprehensive imaging, and coordinate multidisciplinary management to improve patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1195
content: Fracture of the shoulder and upper arm (Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm) and fracture of the upper limb (fracture of upper limb) are both classified as subtypes of a clavicle fracture (كسر الترقوة). The clavicle fracture itself is regarded as a specific form of a more general limb fracture (كسر في الأطراف). Clinical observations indicate that clavicle fractures (كسر الترقوة) frequently co‑occur with femur bone fractures (كسر عظم الفخذ). This hierarchical relationship highlights that injuries to the shoulder, upper arm, and broader upper‑limb region are encompassed within the category of clavicular injuries, which are part of the overall spectrum of limb fractures, and that patients presenting with a clavicle fracture should be evaluated for possible concurrent femoral fractures.

content: Various late effects of bone injuries are directly linked to the underlying fracture. This relationship underscores that regardless of the anatomical region—whether multiple bones, the skull and facial skeleton, the upper or lower extremities, or the femoral neck—post‑traumatic sequelae are fundamentally connected to the original fracture event, highlighting the importance of monitoring and managing these late complications in patient care.
